name: Robert Alter
desc: UC Berkeley professor whose landmark translation and commentary illuminates the narrative art, poetic structure, and rhetorical genius of the Hebrew Bible.

name: A.A. Anderson
desc: British OT scholar. WBC 2 Samuel with philological rigour and text-critical depth.

name: Timothy R. Ashley
desc: Author of the NICOT commentary on Numbers — the standard evangelical-scholarly resource. Combines close Hebrew philology with theological sensitivity and attention to narrative structure.

name: Robert D. Bergen
desc: Professor of Old Testament and Biblical Languages at Hannibal-LaGrange University. NAC commentary on 1–2 Samuel with attention to literary structure and theological themes.

name: Daniel I. Block
desc: Gunther H. Knoedler Professor Emeritus of Old Testament at Wheaton College. Author of the magisterial two-volume NICOT commentary on Ezekiel and NAC Judges commentary. Deep engagement in ANE covenant forms, Deuteronomistic theology, and priestly traditions.

name: John Calvin
desc: Reformer and theologian whose Commentaries remain among the most rigorous exegetical works produced — precise, pastoral, and deeply doctrinal.

name: Catena Aurea
desc: Thomas Aquinas’s “Golden Chain” — a curated anthology of Chrysostom, Augustine, Ambrose, and eighty other Fathers commenting verse by verse on all four Gospels.

name: Peter C. Craigie
desc: NICOT commentary on Deuteronomy. Brings deep knowledge of Hittite treaty structure, Ugaritic literature, and the ethics of holy war to the most theologically demanding Pentateuch book.

name: Richard S. Hess
desc: Distinguished Professor of Old Testament at Denver Seminary. TOTC Joshua commentary grounded in ANE archaeology and ancient Near Eastern textual parallels.

name: David M. Howard Jr.
desc: Professor of Old Testament at Bethel Seminary. NAC Joshua commentary with expertise in the historical books and the Deuteronomistic History.

name: Robert L. Hubbard Jr.
desc: Author of the definitive NICOT commentary on Ruth. Combines rigorous Hebrew philology with deep engagement with ANE legal customs and the book’s hesed theology.

name: Craig S. Keener
desc: Author of the four-volume Acts commentary — the most comprehensive evangelical treatment ever produced. Encyclopedic knowledge of Greco-Roman culture and Jewish diaspora practice.

name: John MacArthur
desc: Verse-by-verse exposition with pastoral application, grounded in grammatical-historical interpretation. The most widely used evangelical study Bible commentator of the late 20th century.

name: Joel Marcus
desc: Author of the two-volume Anchor Bible Commentary on Mark — the most comprehensive critical commentary on the Gospel in English. Dead Sea Scrolls parallels and rigorous Greek philology.

name: Jacob Milgrom
desc: Author of the definitive three-volume Anchor Bible commentary on Leviticus and Numbers. Transformed understanding of the priestly purity system as a coherent moral theology.

name: NET Bible Notes
desc: Over 60,000 translator notes documenting every significant textual and translational decision — making visible the interpretive work embedded in every English rendering.

name: Iain W. Provan
desc: Marshall Sheppard Professor at Regent College. Literary-theological reading of Kings with attention to Deuteronomistic narrative and canonical context.

name: David Rhoads
desc: Pioneer of narrative-critical reading of Mark. Mark as Story (with Dewey and Michie) transformed how scholars approach the Gospel as a unified literary work with deliberate narrative art.

name: A.T. Robertson
desc: Southern Baptist Seminary professor and the greatest NT Greek grammarian in American scholarship. His Word Pictures illuminate what the Greek text is actually doing, verse by verse.

name: Nahum Sarna
desc: JPS Torah Commentary series editor. Brings rabbinic tradition, ancient Near Eastern parallels, and deep Hebrew philology to bear on Genesis and Exodus.

name: Jeffrey H. Tigay
tradition: Jewish Biblical Scholar · JPS Torah Commentary
desc: JPS Torah Commentary on Deuteronomy. Combines philological rigour, rabbinic tradition, and ANE comparative study. The definitive modern Jewish scholarly commentary on Deuteronomy in English.

name: David T. Tsumura
desc: Professor Emeritus at Japan Bible Seminary. NICOT 1 Samuel with specialisation in Semitic languages and Ugaritic parallels to the Hebrew text.

name: Bruce K. Waltke
desc: Co-author of the standard Biblical Hebrew grammar and author of the two-volume NICOT Proverbs commentary. Unmatched in Hebrew philology and canonical-theological reading of wisdom literature.

name: Barry G. Webb
desc: Retired Senior Research Fellow at Moore Theological College, Sydney. NICOT Judges commentary with attention to the book’s literary architecture and theological thesis of progressive deterioration.

name: Donald J. Wiseman
desc: Professor of Assyriology at the University of London. Uniquely combined cuneiform expertise with evangelical biblical scholarship. TOTC 1 and 2 Kings.

name: Martin J. Selman
desc: Director of Postgraduate Studies at Spurgeon’s College, London. TOTC 1 and 2 Chronicles: the Chronicler’s theological agenda made accessible.

name: Sara Japhet
tradition: Critical · OTL I–II Chronicles · Hebrew University
desc: Professor of Bible at Hebrew University of Jerusalem (1934–2022). The definitive critical commentary on Chronicles. Demonstrated the Chronicler’s literary artistry and theological independence.

name: Derek Kidner
desc: Tyndale commentator on Ezra-Nehemiah with clarity and pastoral warmth.
bio: Derek Kidner was a British Old Testament scholar and Warden of Tyndale House, Cambridge. His Tyndale commentaries are models of concise, theologically rich exposition. On Ezra-Nehemiah he combines careful attention to the Persian-period historical context with pastoral sensitivity to the spiritual dynamics of restoration.

name: H.G.M. Williamson
desc: WBC Ezra-Nehemiah with rigorous historical-critical analysis.
bio: Hugh Williamson is Regius Professor of Hebrew at Oxford. His Word Biblical Commentary on Ezra-Nehemiah is the standard academic reference, combining rigorous textual and historical analysis with theological insight. Particularly strong on the Aramaic sections and Persian administrative context.

name: Karen H. Jobes
desc: NIV Application Commentary on Esther, bridging ancient text and modern context.
bio: Karen Jobes is Gerald F. Hawthorne Professor Emerita of New Testament Greek and Exegesis at Wheaton College. Her NIVAC commentary on Esther masterfully balances literary analysis, Persian-period history, and theological application. She is particularly strong on the providence theology of Esther — how God works through human agency without explicit mention.

name: Jon D. Levenson
desc: OTL Esther from Harvard. Jewish academic reading of Esther as diaspora survival narrative.
bio: Jon Levenson is Albert A. List Professor of Jewish Studies at Harvard Divinity School. His OTL commentary on Esther is the standard academic treatment, combining literary sophistication with deep knowledge of Jewish interpretive traditions. He reads Esther as a diaspora survival narrative with profound connections to the Exodus story — Haman as a new Pharaoh, Esther as a new Moses.

name: David J.A. Clines
desc: WBC Job (3 vols). The most comprehensive modern commentary on any biblical book.
bio: David Clines is Emeritus Professor of Biblical Studies at the University of Sheffield. His three-volume Word Biblical Commentary on Job (1989, 2006, 2011) is the most comprehensive modern commentary on the book.

name: Norman C. Habel
desc: OTL Job. Reads Job as a legal drama — cosmic court case ending in theophany.
bio: Norman Habel is an Australian Lutheran theologian whose Old Testament Library commentary on Job (1985) reads the book as a legal drama — a cosmic court case in which Job demands a hearing and receives not a verdict but a theophany.

name: Tremper Longman III
desc: NICOT Ecclesiastes and Song of Solomon. Reformed evangelical with literary sensitivity.
bio: Tremper Longman III is Distinguished Scholar and Professor Emeritus of Biblical Studies at Westmont College. His NICOT commentaries on Ecclesiastes and Song of Solomon combine evangelical conviction with sophisticated literary analysis. He reads Qoheleth as a foil whose pessimism is corrected by the epilogue narrator.

name: Michael V. Fox
desc: JPS Ecclesiastes. The definitive philological commentary on Qoheleth.
works: JPS Qohelet (2004), A Time to Tear Down (1999)
bio: Michael V. Fox is the Jay C. and Ruth Halls Bascom Professor Emeritus of Hebrew at the University of Wisconsin-Madison. His two books on Ecclesiastes represent the most thorough philological analysis of Qoheleth in English. He argues that hebel means “absurd” rather than “meaningless” — life is not empty but irrational, defying human attempts to find coherent patterns.

name: Duane A. Garrett
desc: NAC Song of Songs. Reads it as a unified love poem celebrating marital intimacy.
bio: Duane Garrett is John R. Sampey Professor of Old Testament Interpretation at The Southern Baptist Theological Seminary. His NAC commentary on Song of Songs reads the poem as a celebration of marital love, structured as a unified dramatic sequence rather than a collection of fragments.

name: Willem A. VanGemeren
desc: EBC Psalms. Comprehensive evangelical commentary integrating ANE background with Reformed theology.
bio: Willem VanGemeren is Professor Emeritus of Old Testament and Semitic Languages at Trinity Evangelical Divinity School. His EBC commentary on the Psalms is one of the most thorough evangelical treatments, combining detailed exegesis with sensitivity to the Psalms as worship literature.

name: John Goldingay
desc: BCOT Psalms (3 vols). Literary-theological commentary with fresh translation.
bio: John Goldingay is David Allan Hubbard Professor Emeritus of Old Testament at Fuller Theological Seminary. His three-volume Baker Commentary on Psalms offers fresh translations with literary and theological insight, reading each psalm as both ancient Israelite worship and contemporary spiritual resource.

name: John J. Collins
desc: Leading Daniel scholar; Hermeneia commentary. Expert on Jewish apocalypticism.
bio: John J. Collins is Holmes Professor of Old Testament Criticism and Interpretation at Yale Divinity School. His Hermeneia commentary on Daniel (1993) is the standard critical treatment. He is the foremost authority on Jewish apocalypticism and Second Temple literature. His work situates Daniel within the broader apocalyptic genre of the ancient Near East while taking its theological claims seriously. His Apocalyptic Imagination traces the genre from Daniel through the Dead Sea Scrolls to the New Testament.

name: Adele Berlin
desc: Leading Lamentations scholar; JPS commentary. Expert on biblical poetry and literary criticism.
works: Lamentations (JPS Bible Commentary, 2002), The Dynamics of Biblical Parallelism (1985)
bio: Adele Berlin is Robert H. Smith Professor Emerita at the University of Maryland. Her JPS commentary on Lamentations (2002) combines literary sensitivity with historical awareness. She pioneered the application of modern literary theory to biblical poetry, demonstrating how parallelism, wordplay, and structure create meaning. Her reading of Lamentations attends to both the raw grief and the artful poetic construction that shapes it.

name: Kathleen O'Connor
desc: Lamentations as trauma literature; pioneering grief-theology scholar.
works: Lamentations and the Tears of the World (2002)
bio: Kathleen M. O'Connor is William Marcellus McPheeters Professor Emerita of Old Testament at Columbia Theological Seminary. Her Lamentations and the Tears of the World (2002) reads Lamentations through the lens of trauma theory, arguing that the poems perform the psychological and spiritual work of processing catastrophic loss. She shows how the book's raw honesty — including its anger at God — is itself therapeutic and faithful. Her work has transformed how pastors and theologians use Lamentations in contexts of grief, disaster, and collective trauma.

name: John N. Oswalt
desc: Leading evangelical Isaiah scholar; NICOT commentary (2 vols). Defends unified authorship.
bio: John N. Oswalt is Visiting Distinguished Professor of Old Testament at Asbury Theological Seminary. His two-volume NICOT commentary on Isaiah (1986, 1998) is the standard evangelical treatment, defending unified authorship while engaging critically with the text. He argues that the theological coherence of Isaiah 1-66 is best explained by single authorship. His work combines careful exegesis with pastoral sensitivity.

name: Brevard S. Childs
desc: Pioneer of canonical criticism; reads Isaiah as unified theological whole regardless of authorship.
bio: Brevard Springs Childs was Sterling Professor of Divinity at Yale. His OTL commentary on Isaiah (2001) applies his canonical approach: reading the final form of the text as Scripture, bracketing (without dismissing) historical-critical questions about authorship. For Childs, the canonical shape of Isaiah — judgment (1-39) then comfort (40-66) — is itself theologically significant regardless of how many human authors produced it. His approach bridges conservative and critical scholarship by taking the text seriously as both literature and Scripture.

name: Walther Zimmerli
desc: Swiss Old Testament scholar and professor at the University of Göttingen. Author of the two-volume Hermeneia commentary on Ezekiel, widely regarded as the standard critical commentary. Pioneer of form-critical and tradition-historical analysis of Ezekiel, with particular attention to the recognition formula and the theology of divine presence.

name: Pieter A. Verhoef
desc: South African Old Testament scholar whose NICOT commentaries on Haggai and Malachi combine careful philological analysis with sensitivity to the post-exilic historical setting, covenant theology, and the canonical shape of the Book of the Twelve.

name: Mark J. Boda
desc: Canadian Old Testament scholar and professor at McMaster Divinity College. Author of the NICOT commentary on Zechariah (2016) and co-editor of several volumes on the Book of the Twelve. Boda integrates form-critical analysis with canonical and theological reading, attending carefully to the inner-biblical connections between Zechariah and earlier prophetic traditions, the role of the priestly-prophetic voice, and the eschatological horizon of chapters 9-14.

name: Andrew E. Hill
desc: American Old Testament scholar and professor at Wheaton College. Author of the Anchor Yale Bible commentary on Malachi (1998), which combines detailed philological analysis with attention to the book’s rhetorical structure, its disputational style, and its canonical position as the bridge between the prophetic corpus and the New Testament. Hill is attentive to Malachi’s covenant theology, its critique of priestly corruption, and its eschatological horizon.

name: Walter Brueggemann
desc: William Marcellus McPheeters Professor Emeritus at Columbia Theological Seminary. A towering figure in Old Testament theology whose prophetic-imagination framework reshaped how a generation reads Jeremiah.

name: Jack R. Lundbom
desc: Author of the three-volume Anchor Bible Commentary on Jeremiah, the most comprehensive English-language commentary on the book. Specialist in rhetorical criticism and the structural artistry of Jeremiah’s poetry.
